Event: He takes down a fully-armed War Mûmak and its crew all by himself
Explanation: He risks himself but gains glory/survival while removing a major threat to allies and civilians.

Event: He runs out of arrows only once after killing more orcs than he had arrows
Explanation: His extreme efficiency in combat benefits him and his side with no stated downside beyond a brief ammo limit.

Event: He switches between his bow and knives when appropriate in combat
Explanation: Adaptable weapon use keeps him safer and more lethal for his allies’ cause.

Event: He wields Orcrist when his group captures Thorin and the other dwarves
Explanation: He gains a powerful sword and status while the captured dwarves are disadvantaged and disarmed.

Event: He threatens to kill Thorin during his initial introduction
Explanation: Intimidation asserts dominance and control at Thorin’s expense without clear wider benefit.

Event: Over time he lets his disdain for dwarves end as he spends time with Gimli
Explanation: Abandoning prejudice improves his relationships and strengthens alliance between races.

Event: He originally shares his father's disdain for non-elves, especially dwarves
Explanation: Prejudice reinforces his in-group status but harms others socially and politically.

Event: He reacts with confusion to his first real injury, then goes after Bolg again
Explanation: Quickly overcoming shock and re-engaging improves his battlefield effectiveness for himself and allies.

Event: He calls out his father and people for ignoring the outside world
Explanation: Criticizing isolationism risks disfavor but pushes his society toward more responsible action.

Event: He tells his father he cannot go home and is told to seek “a young ranger by the name of Strider”
Explanation: Leaving home expands his alliances (Aragorn) and ultimately benefits the wider world’s defense.

Event: He warns Tauriel that her love for Kíli can’t work out but does not interfere
Explanation: He protects his own feelings somewhat while offering realistic counsel and respecting her agency.

Event: He feels sorry for Tauriel when Kíli is killed trying to rescue her
Explanation: Empathy sustains their bond and supports her emotionally with no personal cost beyond sadness.

Event: He saves Tauriel from Bolg and kills Bolg after an epic battle
Explanation: He risks himself but preserves Tauriel’s life and removes a powerful enemy.

Event: He is callous and insulting toward dwarves in The Hobbit
Explanation: His rudeness bolsters his ego/species pride while damaging relations with dwarves.

Event: He cares about the world beyond his borders despite elven aloofness
Explanation: Concern for external affairs improves his strategic position and helps other peoples.

Event: He disputes with his father over the course Mirkwood should take after Thorin and Company escape
Explanation: Challenging royal policy risks his standing but pushes toward more engaged, responsible action.

Event: He notes a red sunrise as sign of massacre and dim stars as Sauron’s influence
Explanation: Interpreting omens provides useful information for decision-making at little cost to himself.

Event: He calls out his father’s willingness to ignore what’s happening outside
Explanation: Open criticism endangers his favor at court but urges his father toward wiser policy.

Event: He wins (or nearly wins) a drinking contest with Gimli, barely getting tipsy after nine ales
Explanation: High tolerance and playful rivalry amuse both with no real harm depicted.

Event: He shoots the Uruk Gimli is using as a trophy to argue for a tie in their kill-count contest
Explanation: Competitive teasing slightly benefits his ego while giving Gimli a chance for banter; no serious harm.

Event: He panics when injured and unable to defeat Bolg in single combat
Explanation: His shaken confidence harms his composure and offers no benefit to others, though no clear outside harm is shown.

Event: He clearly fears the Balrog when Gandalf names it
Explanation: His visible fear signals the true threat level, helping the group take it seriously though it stresses him.

Event: He panics when Aragorn falls fighting the armored troll, screaming his friend’s name
Explanation: Emotional reaction momentarily hurts his composure but underscores Aragorn’s importance and may prompt aid.

Event: He draws an arrow on Éomer, warning he’ll die first if he harms Gimli
Explanation: He risks escalation to protect Gimli, but his resolve successfully deters immediate harm to his friend.

Event: He forms an odd friendship with Gimli despite their races’ feud
Explanation: The bond greatly enriches both and helps bridge an ancient interspecies divide.

Event: He racks up an impressive body-count across both trilogies
Explanation: High combat effectiveness keeps him alive and substantially aids his allies.

Event: He realizes his father has flaws, notes Thorin saving his life, and agrees to help Tauriel with the orcs
Explanation: Accepting uncomfortable truths and helping against orcs benefits him morally and materially while aiding others.

Event: He leaves Mirkwood with Tauriel, disobeying his father to fight the darkness
Explanation: He sacrifices obedience and safety for a broader, necessary fight that protects many.

Event: He shield-surfs down the Helm’s Deep stairs, then throws the shield to pin an orc
Explanation: Acrobatic maneuvering increases his mobility and lethality for his side.

Event: He throws Orcrist at a troll to save Thorin’s life
Explanation: Giving up a powerful weapon in the moment preserves Thorin and turns the tide against the troll.

Event: He falls into Tranquil Fury and tries to run Bolg down in retribution, likely toward Dol Guldur’s army
Explanation: Chasing Bolg toward the orc army endangers himself and could compromise his side for mere revenge.

Event: He again enters Tranquil Fury when Éomer threatens to kill Gimli
Explanation: Cold anger gives him resolve to protect his friend and effectively deter the threat.

Event: He delivers a “What the Hell, Hero?” to Thranduil after he decapitates Narzug despite vowing to release him
Explanation: Challenging his father’s broken word risks backlash but upholds integrity and more humane conduct.

Event: He questions Théoden sending untrained old men and boys to fight at Helm’s Deep when speaking to Aragorn
Explanation: Raising ethical and tactical concerns may influence better use of forces without hurting him.

Event: He nails Bolg with a hurricanrana during their duel on the tower bridge
Explanation: Using an unorthodox move improves his odds in the duel and helps remove a dangerous foe.

Event: He tells Thranduil he can’t return to the forest after all that’s passed between them
Explanation: Severing ties costs him home and status but affirms his values and independence, which others may benefit from.
